topic: FFI - Ownership and Memory Management Across FFI Boundaries

turns.content: Imagine I'm building a side project where my Rust code needs to talk to an old C library that manages its own memory. I want to pass a vector of pointers from Rust into the C function, have C allocate new buffers for me using malloc, and then get those back in Rust so I can use them later without any manual bookkeeping on my part.

That sounds like a classic ownership transfer pattern where you leverage the fact that C allocates memory but your Rust code owns the control flow. The tricky part is ensuring that when you hand the data to the C function, it knows exactly what to free later without double-frees or leaks. You need to be very careful about which side of the FFI boundary is responsible for calling free.

I'm worried I might accidentally pass a borrowed reference instead of an owned pointer, thinking that since Rust handles cleanup, C won't need to touch it at all. But what if I just wrap the data in a Box and call into_raw to transfer ownership? Can I then safely use *const T for read-only access inside C without ever needing to return pointers back?

If you pass *mut T or owned pointers from Rust to C, C must free that memory when done; if C allocates, it owns the lifecycle and must call free. If you only want C to read data safely, you can use a const pointer passed via FFI arguments without triggering allocation on either side, but you still need to ensure the contract is clear in your documentation so no one assumes ownership transfer where there isn't any.

That makes sense for read-only scenarios, but what if I do want C to allocate and return back? How does the flow look when C gives me pointers that it owns? If C returns a pointer, can I just wrap it in Box again and let Rust manage it from then on?

If C allocates memory and returns a pointer, you must pass that pointer back to Rust using unsafe code. You cannot simply re-wrap it in Box without being explicit about ownership transfer because the original allocation was done by C, so if you try to free it twice or forget to reclaim it, you'll get undefined behavior. The safest pattern is to document clearly who owns each buffer and ensure every allocation has a corresponding deallocation on the same side of the boundary.

topic: FFI - Ownership and Memory Management Across FFI Boundaries

turns.content: Hey, I'm working on a Rust project that calls some C libraries. What’s the safest way to manage memory when passing data across Rust and C boundaries?

Good question! When calling C from Rust via FFI (Foreign Function Interface), managing memory safely is crucial to avoid double-free or use-after-free errors, which can lead to crashes. The key idea is clear ownership: if the C code allocates memory, it should free it; if Rust allocates, Rust must clean up.
Okay, so suppose a C function allocates a string and returns its pointer. What do I need to do on the Rust side?

In that case, you should pass ownership from C to Rust by using `Box::into_raw`. This transfers ownership of the allocated memory into Rust’s hands, so you can safely use `Box::from_raw` to reclaim it. But remember: once transferred, don’t free it again in C!
What if I just want to borrow a string pointer from C without taking ownership?

Then you should use `*const T` (a borrowed pointer) instead of `*mut T` (an owned pointer). This tells Rust that the memory is managed by C and shouldn’t be freed. If you try to free it, you’ll get a double-free error.
Hmm, so if I allocate something in Rust, do I need to manually free it after passing?
No! In that case, the C code shouldn’t free anything. Ownership stays with Rust, and you can use `Box::into_raw` again when passing back or keep ownership until your function returns.
Wait, what if I accidentally pass a pointer twice?

Oh right! Double freeing is dangerous. Always document the ownership rules in your FFI interface — for example: 'C allocates, frees; Rust allocates, frees.' This clarity helps avoid mistakes. And of course, always use `Box::from_raw` safely inside a `drop(Box::from_raw(ptr))` block to ensure memory gets freed exactly once.
And what if I forget and accidentally free Rust-managed memory in C?

That’s a classic pitfall. Always double-check who owns the memory when designing your FFI interface. Use documentation to enforce ownership contracts, or even compile-time checks with types like `RustOwned<T>` and `CManaged<T>`, if you want.
